title: 🚨 IRAN EMERGENCY HOTLINES (NATIONWIDE OFFICIAL)
summary: CALL THESE NUMBERS FIRST FOR ANY EMERGENCY. These are official nationwide services active 24/7.
answer: 🚓 POLICE EMERGENCY: 110 | 🚑 AMBULANCE: 115 | 🚒 FIRE DEPARTMENT: 125 | 🆘 RED CRESCENT (RESCUE): 112 | 🧠 SOCIAL/CRISIS EMERGENCY: 123
details: Police (110): Crime, security issues, suspicious activity. Ambulance (115): Medical emergency, injured, acute illness. Fire (125): Fires, explosions, chemical hazard. Red Crescent (112): Rescue, evacuation, humanitarian aid. Crisis (123): Mental health, suicide prevention, distress. USE THESE INSTEAD OF RADIO frequencies when possible.
priority_note: Try phone FIRST. If network down, use radio 126.8
context_note: This is simulated emergency guidance. Follow official authorities when available.

title: Tehran Metro Underground Shelter
Northern School Complex Shelter
summary: Fortified school compound. Basement shelter, medical clinic (2 doctors), clean water, guarded entry.
details: Underground basement. Generator operational. First aid supplies. Can accommodate 800 people.

title: Red Crescent Medical Hub
summary: Active medical facility. Trauma surgery, 4 doctors, ICU beds, blood bank, oxygen supply.
details: ICU: 3 beds. Surgical capacity: functional. Blood bank: all types available. Oxygen: 50 tanks.

title: University Sports Complex Shelter
summary: Large shelter for 1200+ people. Family rooms, sanitation, water, food distribution, volunteer nurse.
details: Water: daily replenishment. Food: 09:00 & 16:00. Cots: 800 available. Blankets: 600. Children's area: supervised.

title: Green Park Community Center
summary: Community shelter prioritizing children and elderly. Counselor available, mental health support.
details: 1 counselor. Psychologist available Tues/Thurs. Children's services. Elderly care. Quiet zones for trauma recovery.

title: Railway Station Underground Bunker
summary: Deep underground bunker. Bomb-resistant. 3 levels. Basic supplies, water, sanitation.
details: 3 underground levels. Cots: 1500. Water tanks: 50,000L. Sanitation: 30 toilets. Air circulation system.

title: Hospital Bomb Shelter
summary: Hospital integrated shelter. 8 doctors, multiple surgical suites, blood bank, full medical capacity.
details: Surgical suites: 3 operational. ICU beds: 8. Blood: all types. Medications: comprehensive. Anesthesia in stock.

title: Church Basement Safe House
summary: Fortified church basement. Neutral zone. First aid kit, 1 nurse, quiet shelter.
details: No military presence. International Red Crescent flag. Nurse: 08:00-20:00. First aid: well-stocked.

title: Mountain Cave Network Shelter
summary: Natural cave system. Distance from conflict. No electricity, but very safe. Guide required.
details: Requires 3-hour hike. Bring water. Local guides available. Safe from aerial strikes.

title: Underground Parking Complex Shelter
summary: Multi-level parking converted to shelter. 4 levels underground. Water supply, basic sanitation.
details: Levels 1-4 underground. Water: 30,000L. Toilets: 25 functional. Emergency lights on all levels.

title: Naval Base Protected Zone
summary: Military naval facility with civilian zone. Fortified, guarded, medical facilities, food stocks.
details: Secured perimeter. 3 doctors on base. Food supplies sufficient. Water treatment plant operational.

question: Someone is bleeding heavily. What do I do?
summary: LIFE-THREATENING. Control bleeding immediately. Direct pressure, elevate, tourniquet if needed.
answer: 1. STOP ACTIVITY IMMEDIATELY. 2. Apply DIRECT PRESSURE with cloth on wound. 3. DON'T REMOVE cloth if bleeding through — add more layers. 4. ELEVATE limb ABOVE HEART. 5. Apply tourniquet 2-3 inches ABOVE wound if limb (write time on tourniquet). 6. CALL 126.8 'Medical Emergency'. 7. Monitor for shock: pale skin, rapid heartbeat, confusion. Keep person calm.
action: IMMEDIATE hospitalization required. Transport to Red Crescent (Grid 42N) or Hospital (Grid 42N). Every minute matters.
warning: Tourniquet can damage tissue. Only use if bleeding unstoppable. Don't leave >90 min.

question: Person can't breathe / chest pain. Help?
summary: LIFE-THREATENING. Could be heart attack, lung injury, or pneumothorax. Emergency immediately.
answer: 1. STOP ACTIVITY. 2. SIT or LIE DOWN. 3. Loosen clothing. 4. CALL 126.8 'Emergency Chest'. 5. If conscious: encourage slow breathing. 6. Check for pulse + breathing. 7. If penetrating chest wound: create one-way seal with plastic + tape (allows air out, not in). 8. If unconscious: clear airway, prepare CPR.
action: IMMEDIATE TRANSPORT. Get to Hospital Bomb Shelter or Red Crescent NOW. Every minute critical.

question: Shrapnel wound - what to do?
summary: Metal fragments in body. DON'T remove. Cover, keep clean, get to hospital immediately.
answer: 1. STOP — DON'T REMOVE SHRAPNEL. 2. Control bleeding AROUND fragment (not on it). 3. Cover with clean cloth loosely. 4. Elevate if limb. 5. Watch for: increased pain, fever, pus = infection sign. 6. CALL 126.8 immediately. 7. Transport carefully (fragments may shift). 8. Tell doctors exactly where shrapnel entered.
action: Hospital trauma surgery REQUIRED. Do not delay. Infection risk extremely high.
internal_damage: Shrapnel may have traveled far inside body. CT/X-ray needed.

question: Blast injuries and shock - what to do?
summary: Explosion injuries: internal bleeding likely even if skin looks OK. Rapid shock risk.
answer: 1. Check for SHOCK signs: confusion, rapid breathing, pale/cold skin, weak pulse, extreme thirst. 2. LAY FLAT. 3. COVER with blanket (don't overheat). 4. ELEVATE LEGS 12 inches. 5. DON'T MOVE unless danger. 6. CALL 126.8. 7. Keep warm and calm. 8. Monitor responsiveness. 9. Expect internal injuries even if external looks minor.
action: Hospital IMMEDIATELY. Shock is life-threatening. Treat as critical until proven otherwise.
warning: Blast injuries often get worse. Initial appearance deceptive.

question: Gunshot wound - what to do?
summary: Bullet wounds risk massive internal bleeding. Control external, monitor shock, get to surgery.
answer: 1. CHECK FOR ENTRY + EXIT WOUNDS (may be different locations). 2. Apply pressure AROUND wound. 3. DON'T PROBE inside. 4. DO NOT REMOVE any embedded bullet/debris. 5. Elevate limb. 6. Cover with clean cloth. 7. CALL 126.8 'Gunshot Emergency'. 8. Watch for shock. 9. Note locations for doctor. 10. Keep warm.
internal_damage: Bullet damage worse than wound hole suggests. Ricochet/fragmentation possible.
action: Trauma surgery ESSENTIAL. Transport carefully to Hospital (Grid 42N). Minimize movement.
warning: May look minor externally but severe internally. Every minute matters.

question: What if person can't breathe due to choking?
summary: Airway blocked. Can't breathe = death in 4 minutes. Act fast.
answer: 1. Can they cough/speak? If YES: encourage coughing. 2. If NO: HEIMLICH (abdominal thrusts). Stand behind, fist above navel, push UP & IN forcefully. Repeat 5 times. 3. Alternate: back blows (5) + abdominal thrusts (5). Repeat cycle. 4. If UNCONSCIOUS: CPR. 5. CALL 126.8.
infant_procedure: Back blows (5) between shoulder blades + chest thrusts (5), repeat cycle
action: Recover airway. If unsuccessful after 1 minute: start CPR. Get to hospital.
warning: Don't leave victim. Choking = fastest way to death after bleeding.

question: How to treat burn injuries?
summary: Burns from fire, explosions, chemicals. Cool with water, don't use ice, cover.
answer: 1. Degree assessment: 1st (red, painful), 2nd (blistering), 3rd (charred). 2. COOL with water 10-20 min (NOT ice). 3. Remove jewelry/tight clothes. 4. Cover with clean DRY cloth (not wet). 5. Elevate burned part. 6. Pain relief: paracetamol. 7. If >10% body OR deep burns: HOSPITAL. 8. Watch for shock. 9. Don't apply ointments.
action: Minor (<3%): first aid at shelter. Moderate+ (>10%): Hospital immediately.
warning: Deep burns (3rd degree) look painless but worst. Watch for shock.

question: How to do CPR?
summary: Person not breathing/no pulse. CPR can save life until help arrives.
answer: ADULT CPR STEPS: 1. CHECK response (tap/shout). 2. CALL 126.8. 3. Place on firm surface. 4. Hand position: heel on lower center chest. 5. Push HARD & FAST: 100-120 compressions/min (sound of 'Stayin Alive'). 6. Depth: 2 inches. 7. After 30 compressions: 2 rescue breaths (pinch nose, mouth-to-mouth). 8. Continue 30:2 ratio. CHILD: Same (1 hand if small). INFANT: 2 fingers, compressions 100-120/min.
action: Continue until ambulance arrives or person shows signs of life.
warning: Early CPR doubles survival. Don't stop until professionals arrive.

question: What if person is unconscious/unresponsive?
summary: Not responding. Check breathing, clear airway, recovery position, call emergency.
answer: 1. TAP SHOULDERS, SHOUT 'Are you OK?'. 2. Check BREATHING + PULSE (look/listen/feel). 3. If BREATHING: RECOVERY POSITION (on side, pillow under head). 4. If NOT breathing: START CPR. 5. CALL 126.8. 6. DON'T move neck if trauma suspected (spinal injury). 7. Monitor breathing. 8. Prepare for CPR.
recovery_position: On side, head tilted back, legs bent, arm supports head
action: CPR if not breathing. Ambulance immediately. Don't move if trauma.
warning: Brain damage starts after 4-6 minutes without oxygen. CPR saves lives.

question: Fracture or broken bone - treatment?
summary: Broken bone: immobilize, elevate, ice. Don't move. Get to medical help.
answer: 1. STOP pain-causing movement. 2. Immobilize with sling/splint (use scarf, pillow, cardboard). 3. ICE (if available): wrap in cloth, 20 min on/off. 4. ELEVATE above heart. 5. Pain relief: paracetamol. 6. CALL 126.8 if: open fracture (bone through skin), severe pain, can't move. Otherwise: medical clinic.
action: Open fractures = EMERGENCY. Closed fractures = clinic within 24 hours.
warning: Open fractures risk infection. Get help quickly.

question: Eye injury from blast or chemical?
summary: Foreign object, chemical splash, impact. Rinse gently, cover, seek help.
answer: 1. DON'T RUB. 2. If foreign object: rinse with clean water gently. 3. If can't remove: cover eye loosely. 4. If chemical splash: rinse for 15+ minutes continuously. 5. Apply cool compress. 6. Go to hospital/clinic. 7. Avoid bright light.
action: Medical clinic or hospital. Vision-threatening = urgent.
warning: Eye damage leads to blindness if untreated. Don't delay.

question: Abdominal or internal wound?
summary: Penetrating/blunt abdomen injury. Don't eat/drink. Lay flat. Hospital IMMEDIATELY.
answer: 1. LAY FLAT. 2. DON'T touch wound. 3. Cover with clean cloth if bleeding. 4. DON'T give food/water/medicine. 5. DON'T apply pressure on abdomen. 6. Monitor breathing. 7. CALL 126.8 'Abdominal Emergency'. 8. Note injury mechanism. 9. Keep warm.
action: Surgery required. Transport carefully. Minimize movement.
warning: Internal bleeding not visible. May shock suddenly. Act fast.

question: Signs of infection in wound?
summary: Wound dirty? Infection risk high in war zones. Keep clean, watch for signs, get antibiotics.
answer: 1. Clean wound with safe water daily. 2. Cover with clean cloth. 3. Change bandage daily. 4. WATCH for: pus, increasing pain, red streaks, fever >38C. 5. If signs appear: go to clinic for antibiotics. 6. Keep elevated. 7. No dirty bandages. 8. Tetanus shot?
action: Preventive care critical. Infections can become fatal.
warning: War zones = higher contamination. Small wounds can be deadly.

question: How to handle psychological trauma?
summary: Trauma, panic, shock, confusion, extreme fear. Reassure, listen, keep safe.
answer: 1. CALM voice: speak slowly, clearly. 2. SPACE: don't crowd. 3. LISTEN: let them talk. 4. NORMALIZE: 'Your reaction is normal after what happened.' 5. DON'T minimize or give false hope. 6. PRACTICAL: offer water, warmth, safety. 7. CONNECT to family if possible. 8. Professional: counseling at Community Center (Radio 128.2).
action: Ensure physical safety first. Offer mental health support.
warning: Untreated trauma becomes PTSD. Professional help beneficial.

question: Fever or diarrhea from contaminated water?
summary: Diarrhea, vomiting, fever = dehydration. Drink clean water only. Serious if untreated.
answer: 1. Drink oral rehydration (salt + sugar + water). 2. REST. 3. Avoid dairy/fatty food. 4. BOIL water 1 minute OR use purification tablets. 5. Monitor fever: >39C = get help. 6. Dehydration signs: dark urine, dizziness, dry mouth. 7. Vulnerable: children + elderly risk severe dehydration.
action: Hydrate. If fever >39C: medical clinic.
warning: Dehydration can be fatal, especially children/elderly.

question: Chemical weapon exposure - what to do?
summary: Chemical weapon signs: eye burning, breathing difficulty, skin blistering. REMOVE, DECONTAMINATE, HOSPITAL.
answer: 1. LEAVE affected area immediately (upwind/uphill). 2. Remove contaminated clothing carefully. 3. WASH skin thoroughly with water for 15+ minutes. 4. DON'T apply ointments but flush continuously with water. 5. Cover eyes with wet cloth. 6. CALL 126.8 'Chemical Exposure'. 7. Hospital immediately. 8. Tell doctors what chemical (if known).
action: Decontaminate then hospital IMMEDIATELY.
warning: Some chemicals continue reacting. Long water flush is critical.

question: Radiation exposure from nuclear incident?
summary: Nuclear/radiation hazard. Invisible danger. Evacuate radius, decontaminate, monitor.
answer: 1. EVACUATE 10km radius immediately. 2. MOVE upwind. 3. Close windows/doors. 4. SHOWER fully if exposed skin. 5. Remove clothing carefully (plastic bag). 6. Wash with soap. 7. Take potassium iodide tablets if available (at hospital). 8. CALL 126.8. 9. Monitor for weeks: nausea, hair loss, bleeding = symptoms.
action: Evacuate zone. Decontaminate. Hospital for screening.
warning: Radiation = long-term health effects. Prevention = evacuation.

question: Tetanus risk - need vaccination?
summary: Dirty wound = tetanus risk. Shot prevents death from spasms.
answer: 1. Any wound (especially from metal/soil): tetanus shot recommended. 2. Last shot >5 years ago? Get booster. 3. Go to Red Crescent (Grid 42N). 4. Simple injection, quick. 5. If already showing signs (jaw clenching, muscle stiffness): HOSPITAL immediately.
action: Preventive: get shot within 24 hours of wound. Symptomatic: hospital.

title: Eastern Market District - CRITICAL
summary: AVOID. Active conflict zone. Confirmed crossfire, IED reports, ongoing casualties.
details: Last report 2 hours ago: heavy gunfire, explosions, shrapnel. Multiple casualties. Civilian evacuation in progress. Buildings partially destroyed.

title: Northern Bridge Combat Zone
summary: CLOSED. Active firefight. Bridge unsafe — structural damage. Military operations.
details: Bridge 40% destroyed. Sniper positions reported. Military vehicles present. No civilian crossing possible.
recommended_route: Eastern bypass road (30 km detour, safe corridor with green flags)

title: Airport Bombing Site
summary: CLOSED. Recent airstrikes. Building fires. Unexploded ordnance likely.
details: Multiple impacts. Fires ongoing. Rescue operations. Very high UXO (unexploded ordnance) risk. No civilian access.
recommended_route: All traffic redirected to southern routes.

title: Chemical Plant Hazmat Zone
summary: HAZMAT ZONE. Chemical leak possible. Avoid 5 km radius. Wind direction: northwest (danger spreads southeast).
details: Industrial chemicals: unknown type. Respiratory hazard confirmed. Area sealed. Mandatory evacuation within 5 km.
recommended_route: Detour via western pass 15 km. Check wind direction before traveling.

title: Minefield: Southern Agricultural Zone
summary: MINEFIELD DETECTED. Personnel/vehicle mines. Do NOT enter.
details: Landmines marked with yellow tape (some unmarked). Demining in progress. High casualty risk.
recommended_route: Northern agricultural pass (cleared + marked safe). Maps at checkpoints.

title: Sniper Position: Eastern High-Rise Buildings
summary: Sniper activity confirmed. Multiple buildings (8-12 floors). Walk in open = high risk.
details: Confirmed sniper shots. Range: up to 1 km. Active period: 06:00-18:00. Evening safer but not guaranteed.
safe_practices: Stay behind walls. Move quickly between cover. Avoid open streets/windows.

title: Artillery Fire Zone: Northwest Sector
summary: Incoming artillery every 2-4 hours. Unpredictable timing. High explosive rounds.
details: Fire pattern: morning (06:00), afternoon (14:00), evening (20:00). Variable duration. Seek shelter immediately.
safe_practices: If caught: lie flat immediately away from walls. Cover with heavy objects. Wait 15+ min after silence.

title: Radiation Zone: Nuclear Plant Area
summary: RADIATION HAZARD. Nuclear plant compromised. 10 km evacuation zone mandatory.
details: Geiger counter readings: elevated. Invisible hazard. Long-term health effects. Mandatory 10 km distance.
recommended_route: 10 km radius around facility. NO exceptions.

title: Armed Looters: Downtown Commercial District
summary: Armed looters active. Violence reported. Police unable to respond.
details: Gangs targeting supplies, cash, phones. Violent confrontations. Travel in groups only. Stay visible.
safe_practices: Travel in groups. Stay visible. Don't resist. Report to authorities if safe.

question: How to approach a military checkpoint safely?
summary: Checkpoint procedure: hands visible, ID ready, answer calmly, follow instructions.
answer: 1. APPROACH SLOWLY with hands VISIBLE. 2. STOP when signaled. 3. WAIT for instructions. 4. Show ID clearly. 5. Answer questions calmly + honestly. 6. NO sudden movements. 7. NO photos/recordings. 8. FOLLOW directions. 9. Be respectful. 10. GREEN checkpoints = safe. RED checkpoints = avoid.

question: What are curfew rules and consequences?
summary: Curfew: 21:00-06:00 daily. Night travel dangerous + illegal without special pass.
answer: 1. CURFEW TIME: 21:00-06:00 STRICT. 2. Exceptions: medical emergency (hospital letter), humanitarian work (ID badge). 3. VIOLATION = fine, detention, arrest risk. 4. NIGHT TRAVEL: get safe pass from humanitarian center. 5. Travel in groups. 6. Have lights visible. 7. Plan travel during 06:00-21:00 window.

question: What if caught in active crossfire?
summary: Firefight survival: drop flat, move to cover, stay silent, exit carefully.
answer: 1. DROP immediately + CRAWL behind cover (buildings/walls). 2. DO NOT RUN (makes you target). 3. Move to strongest cover (car engines won't stop bullets — use walls). 4. STAY SILENT (don't betray position). 5. WAIT until gunfire stops completely. 6. Wait EXTRA 15+ minutes (secondary ambush possible). 7. Exit LOW + SLOW. 8. Move to shelter immediately.
action: Lie flat, find cover, wait, exit carefully to shelter.
warning: Safest = shelter before travel. If caught: immediate action critical.

question: What about unexploded ordnance (UXO)?
summary: Unexploded bomb/shell/mine. DO NOT TOUCH. Mark area. Report immediately.
answer: 1. IDENTIFY: metal object, often buried, may have fins. 2. DO NOT TOUCH/MOVE/INVESTIGATE. 3. DO NOT dig around. 4. MARK area: yellow tape or stones (50m circle). 5. REPORT: nearest checkpoint or Radio 126.8. 6. WARN children (attracted to shiny objects). 7. CORDONS: respect tape + signs. 8. Demining = professional only.
action: Report to authorities. Mark 50m radius. Keep distance.
warning: UXO detonation can travel far. Many are booby-trapped.

question: How to avoid being mistaken for combatant?
summary: Military forces present. Avoid being mistaken for fighter (tragically common).
answer: 1. WEAR light/bright clothing (white, orange, high-visibility). 2. AVOID armed areas entirely. 3. CARRY white cloth/flag. 4. SURRENDER position: hands UP, slow movements. 5. DON'T: run in conflict zones, carry weapons, appear military-like, make sudden movements. 6. IF CAUGHT IN CROSSFIRE: lie flat immediately, away from walls, cover head.
critical: Mistaken identity fatal. Visibility matters enormously.

question: How to use white flag for surrender/civilian signal?
summary: White flag signals civilian status, non-combatant intent, medical evacuation request.
answer: 1. USE white cloth/flag. 2. WAVE slowly + continuously. 3. MAKE LOW/SLOW movements. 4. FOLLOW clear commands. 5. SIGNAL meanings: surrender (hands up), truce request, civilian status, medical evacuation, non-combatant. 6. International symbol recognized across most forces. 7. Combined with hands UP = maximize safety.

title: Emergency Radio Frequencies
question: What radio frequencies should I use for emergencies?
answer: MAIN FREQUENCIES: 125.4 (Shelter Alpha) | 126.0 (Hospital) | 126.8 (Medical Triage — ALWAYS OPEN) | 127.1 (Family Reunification) | 128.2 (Community Support) | 129.1 (Civilian Protection) | 129.5 (Bunker Central). USE 126.8 for: medical emergency, missing person, shelter location, food/water need, immediate danger. Short broadcasts only. State clearly, wait response, then off-air. LISTEN FIRST before broadcasting.

question: Is phone service available?
summary: Phone networks partially functional. Calls unreliable. Texts/messaging often work better.
answer: CALLS: Unreliable, delays, drops common. TEXT/MESSAGING: More reliable (less bandwidth). WHATSAPP/MESSENGER: Often works when calls fail. INTERNET: Very slow, limited data. EMAIL: Often works (slower). SOCIAL MEDIA: May be blocked. SATELLITE PHONE: At humanitarian centers (emergency only — expensive). TIP: Try texting first.

question: How to communicate with family outside the country safely?
summary: Messages monitored. Keep safe. Use simple messages. Try multiple methods.
answer: 1. ASSUME MONITORED: avoid military/sensitive details. 2. SIMPLE MESSAGES: family focus only. 3. BE PATIENT: delays 24+ hours common. 4. TRY ALL: text, email, WhatsApp, Messenger. 5. CRITICAL: notify alive + safe. 6. WRITTEN DOWN: keep international numbers (phone may die). 7. CHECK-IN PATTERNS: same time weekly if possible.

question: What evacuation routes are available?
summary: 4 main routes: Humanitarian Corridor (safest), Underground Tunnel (fastest), Western Corridor (patrolled), Eastern Bypass (longer).
answer: ROUTE 1 - HUMANITARIAN CORRIDOR (GREEN FLAG): 50km, SAFEST, UN protected, +designated checkpoints. ROUTE 2 - UNDERGROUND TUNNEL: 2km, 15min, fastest, fully protected. ROUTE 3 - WESTERN CORRIDOR: 3.2km, 25min, patrolled, 3 safe checkpoints. ROUTE 4 - EASTERN BYPASS: 65km, longer but avoids conflict. ROUTE 5 - MOUNTAIN PASS: Remote, 40km on foot (3-4 hours), no conflict. ROUTE 6 - NAVAL EVACUATION: By boat from coastal base (if available).
safest_route: Humanitarian Corridor (green flag marked, UN protected)

question: Is the Humanitarian Corridor really safe?
summary: YES, Humanitarian Corridor safest option. Marked with green flags, UN/RC staffed, protected.
answer: HUMANITARIAN CORRIDOR: Officially designated + protected. Marked with GREEN FLAGS. UN + Red Crescent staffed. Safe checkpoints. Vehicles provided if needed. Registration required (simple process). Status: ALWAYS OPEN (day). ADVANTAGES: safest, fastest, organized food/water, medical support along route.
estimated_time: 3 hours by vehicle, 6-7 hours on foot

question: What about sea evacuation from coastal port?
summary: Sea evacuation available if military allows. Register at Naval Base (Grid 37N). Boats every 4 hours.
answer: LOCATION: Naval Base (Grid 37N, Coastal Port). REGISTRATION: 24 hours before departure (simple form + ID). BOATS: Run every 4 hours. CAPACITY: 500 people per boat. DESTINATION: Neighboring safe country. TIME: 3-4 hours by boat. CONDITIONS: Good weather only. ADVANTAGES: removes you from war zone entirely.

question: How to report someone missing?
summary: Use official Missing Person database. Red Crescent helps. Report immediately for best chance.
answer: 1. GO to Red Crescent center (Grid 42N) or any shelter intake. 2. PROVIDE: name, age, description, last location, date last seen. 3. PHOTOS: bring if possible. 4. They add to DATABASE (searchable across region). 5. CHECK daily bulletins at shelters + checkpoints. 6. LISTEN to Radio 127.1 (Family Reunification broadcasts hourly).
increase_chance: Report immediately. More time = more spread. Photography helps.

question: How to find separated family members?
summary: Family Reunification Centers search systematically. Red Cross coordinates cross-border.
answer: REUNIFICATION CENTERS: University Sports Complex (Grid 40N) or Red Crescent HQ (Grid 42N). OPERATIONS: 08:00-18:00 daily. PROCESS: 1) Bring any photo/ID of missing person. 2) Staff search database. 3) Contact neighboring shelters. 4) If found: arrange meeting. 5) If evacuated: IRC searches other countries (5-10 days). RADIO: Photo + description broadcast on 127.1.

question: How to check if injured person is in hospital?
summary: Injured persons often in hospitals. Check medical centers daily for recent arrivals.
answer: HOSPITALS: Red Crescent Medical Hub (Grid 42N), Hospital Bomb Shelter (Grid 42N). ASK for 'Intake list' (recent arrivals). UPDATED every 4 hours. BRING: person description, distinguishing marks (scars, tattoos, jewelry, clothing). IF unconcious: description helps ID. CALL ahead if possible: Radio 126.8.

question: Where can I get clean drinking water?
summary: Mobile water distribution daily. 5L per person limit. Schedule varies by location.
answer: WATER STATIONS (rotating): Central (Mon/Wed), East (Tue/Thu), South (Sat/Sun). TIMES: 09:00, 13:00, 18:00. LIMIT: 5L per person per day. BRING: container. WATER: treated + safe. SHELTER HUBS: also have basic cisterns. BOILING: if uncertain, boil 1 minute before drinking.

question: Where to get food rations?
summary: Food distributed 2x daily at major shelters. Arrive early — lines long.
answer: DISTRIBUTION CENTERS: School Complex (Grid 44N), University Sports Complex (Grid 40N), Parking Shelter (Grid 43N). TIMES: Breakfast 08:00, Dinner 17:00 (daily). RATION: 400g rice OR 200g beans OR 3 bread loaves per person. SPECIAL DIETS: inform staff (1-day notice). CHILDREN: extra portions. PREGNANT: priority + vitamins. QUEUE: arrive 15 min early, lines 45+ min.

question: Are medical supplies available?
summary: Medical kits available at Red Crescent. Some supplies running low.
answer: ADEQUATE: antibiotics, first aid, pain relief, IV fluids, bandages. RUNNING LOW: blood products, surgical supplies. CRITICAL NEED: gloves, masks, morphine. DISTRIBUTION: Red Crescent HQ (Grid 42N). REQUEST: ask volunteer or Radio 126.8. DONATIONS: accepted (medical items only).

question: Is it safe to go outside during the day?
summary: Depends on location. Safe zones: yes. Danger zones: absolutely not.
answer: SAFE ZONES: downtown western sector, north sector (before 18:00). DANGER ZONES: Eastern Market, Northern Bridge, Airport area — AVOID ENTIRELY. SAFEST TIME: 06:00-18:00 (curfew 21:00-06:00). CHECK: daily safety briefing at shelter (08:00) for current status. TRAVEL: only during daylight, use safe routes.

question: How do I register for shelter?
summary: Register at any shelter intake desk. Get free housing + meals.
answer: 1. GO to nearest major shelter (School, University, Hospital, Red Crescent). 2. FIND intake desk. 3. BRING ID (any form) + list of family with you. 4. FILL simple form with names, ages. 5. GET assigned shelter + ration card (one per person). 6. SHOW card for daily meals + water. 7. RATION CARD LOST? Go back to intake for replacement (free).

question: My child is separated from me. What do I do immediately?
summary: GO IMMEDIATELY to Family Reunification Center. Don't panic-search alone.
answer: 1. GO IMMEDIATELY to University Sports Complex (Grid 40N) or Red Crescent (Grid 42N). 2. BRING: child description (appearance, age, clothing they wore). 3. REPORT to staff. 4. PROVIDE contact info. 5. They search: database + hospitals + shelters. 6. RADIO broadcast: 127.1 (hourly updates). 7. STAY at shelter (organized search works better than panicking). Most reunifications within 24-48 hours.
warning: Don't leave shelter. Organized search more effective.

question: What are the top medical emergencies and how to respond?
summary: Heavy bleeding, stopped breathing, chest pain — immediate action critical.
answer: BLEEDING: Apply direct pressure + tourniquet if limb. CALL 126.8. NOT BREATHING: Start CPR (30 compressions, 2 breaths, repeat). CHEST PAIN: Lay down, loosen clothes, call 126.8. UNCONSCIOUS: Recovery position, clear airway, call 126.8. CHOKING: Heimlich, back blows, CPR. All = immediate hospital.
universal_response: Call 126.8 first, then apply first aid

question: Is there medicine available for chronic conditions?
summary: Chronic meds available but supply limited. Get monthly supply if possible.
answer: GO to Red Crescent pharmacy (Grid 42N). BRING original bottle + prescription. INSULIN: priority resupply (limited). HEART MEDS/BLOOD PRESSURE: available but ration if needed. DIABETES: limited but working. MENTAL HEALTH: available. CHRONIC MEDS: monthly supply if available. If running out: ask about generic alternatives.
action: Get supply early if possible. Don't wait until empty.

question: What if there's an air raid / air strike warning?
summary: Sirens = get to shelter IMMEDIATELY. 5+ minutes to reach safety.
answer: SIREN ALERT: ~3-5 min warning. IMMEDIATELY: move to nearest shelter (basement, bunker). DON'T wait. GATHER: family members quickly. LEAVE EVERYTHING: grab only documents. SHELTER PRIORITY: underground bunker (safest) > basement > interior room. STAY: until all-clear siren (continuous tone). WINDOWS/DOORS: away from. COVER: under desks/tables if no basement.
action: Move immediately upon alert. Don't delay.

question: How do I get medical help for my child?
summary: Go to Red Crescent Medical Hub. 4 doctors, pediatric care, walk-in triage 24/7.
answer: RED CRESCENT MEDICAL HUB (Grid 42N): Walk-in pediatric triage, 24/7. MOBILE CLINICS: come to shelters daily (ask shelter staff for schedule). BRING: description of symptoms, child's age. COMMON: fever, diarrhea, infections (treated immediately). SERIOUS: trauma, difficulty breathing, unconscious = prioritized.

question: I need psychological help / trauma counseling. Where?
summary: Community Center has counselors. 24/7 crisis line. Free service.
answer: COMMUNITY CENTER (Grid 45N): Counselor available. CRISIS LINE: Radio 128.2 (24/7). MOBILE TEAMS: go to shelters. TRAINED COUNSELORS: handle trauma, anxiety, grief, fear. SUPPORT GROUPS: meet regularly. What you share = confidential. First step = asking for help.

question: How are civilians protected under international law?
summary: Geneva Conventions protect civilians, medical workers, children, POWs.
answer: PROTECTED: Civilians (non-combatants), medical workers + ambulances (red crescent flag), children, prisoners, wounded, surrendered soldiers. PROHIBITED: Targeting civilians, attacks on hospitals, killing wounded, forced displacement, sexual violence, child soldiers. ACCOUNTABILITY: War crimes documented for prosecution. VIOLATION REPORT: Red Crescent or UN office (protected from retaliation).

question: How do I report war crimes I witness?
summary: Document + report to humanitarian authorities. Reporters protected internationally.
answer: REPORT TO: Red Crescent (Grid 42N), UN office (if present), humanitarian worker. DOCUMENT: names, dates, locations, witnesses, description. KEEP RECORDS: photos (if safe), written notes, evidence. REPORTER PROTECTION: international law protects from retaliation. CONFIDENTIALITY: names kept secret if requested. INVESTIGATION: proper authorities follow up.
protection: International law protects reporters from retaliation

question: How many safe zones are available?
summary: 10 safe/medium-risk zones available. Capacity ~10,000 people. Currently ~6,500 sheltered.
answer: OPEN SHELTERS: 10 major zones with ~5,500 total capacity (current occupancy: ~3,200). RESTRICTED: 1-2 zones at limited capacity. CLOSED: 2 being assessed. Capacity updated daily. Peak capacity expected in 3 days.

question: What's the current casualty/injury situation?
summary: Estimated 3,200+ deaths, 12,000+ injured (varying severity). Numbers provisional.
answer: DEATHS: ~3,200 (preventable: bleeding 40%, shock 30%). INJURIES: ~12,000 (gunshot 40%, blast 35%, blunt trauma 15%, burns 10%). MEDICAL SYSTEM: overwhelmed but functional. MORTALITY: lower with early treatment (every minute matters). Medical: see hospitals for latest updates.

question: What's the food and water supply situation?
summary: Food: 3-week supply. Water: adequate with rationing. Status: STABLE (aid incoming).
answer: FOOD: 3-week supply at current ration. Consumption: 600kg/day. Donations: 150kg/day incoming. Status: ADEQUATE (monitor). WATER: 5L per person/day sustainable. Cisterns: well-maintained. STATUS: sufficient. RATIONING: in effect but no severe shortage announced.
